summary: Gruber argues that Apple Watch represents Apple's deliberate entry into the luxury watch market, not the smartwatch market, and that its pricing will reflect traditional watchmaking tiers rather than tech industry expectations. He predicts the gold Edition models will start at $5,000 or more based on gold scrap value alone and comparisons to Rolex pricing. He contends the tech press will completely misunderstand Apple's positioning, mistaking it for Vertu-style excess when it's actually disruptively priced for the luxury watch world. The piece also grapples with the fundamental tension of a multi-thousand dollar device built on technology that becomes obsolete in years, unlike mechanical watches that last lifetimes. Gruber ultimately sees Apple Watch as Apple's first product outside the computer industry entirely, signaling a company that has outgrown consumer electronics.
key_points: Apple Watch pricing will follow luxury watch conventions, not tech conventions — the gold Edition will start at $5,000+ based on gold value and market comparisons to Rolex
The tech industry will fundamentally misunderstand Apple Watch because they'll evaluate it as a smartwatch rather than as a luxury timepiece, mistaking Apple for Vertu
The core tension of Apple Watch is that it's a multi-thousand dollar luxury item built on technology with a lifespan of only a few years, unlike mechanical watches that become heirlooms
Apple Watch represents Apple outgrowing the computer industry — using miniature computing technology to disrupt non-tech industries, starting with watches
The egalitarian Warhol/Coke dynamic still holds: the $349 Sport delivers the same Coke, just in a less expensive goblet
money_quotes: Apple Watch is not a product from a tech company, and it will not be understood, at all, by the tech world.
They're going to mistake Apple for Vertu.
There's a massive pricing umbrella in the luxury watch world, and Apple is aiming to take advantage of it.
The Apple Watch only works for Apple if it is, in some sense, the best watch in the world. Not the best smartwatch. That's not enough. The best watch, period.
What's the more ostentatious purchase — a $20,000 Rolex that will last a lifetime, or a $5,000 Apple Watch Edition that will be technically obsolete in four years?
Apple Watch isn't merely Apple's foray into the watch industry — it's their foray outside the computer/consumer electronics industry.
I think the steel and gold Apple Watches are not better-tasting Cokes. They're the same Coke that everyone can get with the $349 Apple Watch Sport, but served in expensive goblets.
I do not think Apple is in deep trouble.
key_insight: Apple Watch is Apple's first product positioned entirely outside the tech industry, entering the luxury watch market at prices that will shock tech observers but represent disruptive value in the world of fine timepieces.
